title: Tutor Finder
tagline: An academic demonstrator that pairs students with tutors through a weighted matching algorithm — and proves data structures are a UX feature, not just a coursework requirement.

screenshot.alt: Tutor Finder search and filtering interface

aboutHeadline: Data-structures coursework that pretends it's a product.
blurb: Tutor Finder is a JavaFX desktop application that pairs students with tutors based on subject, availability, location, and rating. Under the hood it's a data-structures demonstrator — heaps, priority queues, and hash maps doing the work of a matching engine — wrapped in a UI that hides every algorithm behind an experience that feels like a normal search-and-filter flow. The point was to prove that 'data structures' aren't a coursework abstraction; they're how the product knows who to surface first.
pullQuotes: The data structures aren't the project. The match they produce is.
A priority queue, when you can't see it, just feels like good UX.
Algorithms earn their place by disappearing.

highlights.title: Landing & navigation
highlights.lede: The on-ramp into the matching flow.
highlights.body: A landing page with a navigation pane that routes students and tutors into different flows from the start. Shared visual primitives keep both sides feeling like one app even though the surfaces underneath them are very different.

highlights.title: Student & tutor registration
highlights.body: Students and tutors register through dedicated JavaFX flows but write into a shared SQL backend. Tutor records carry subject, availability, location, and rating; student records carry preferences. The matching engine reads from both as a single graph.

highlights.title: Weighted pairing algorithm
highlights.lede: Where the data structures earn their keep.
highlights.body: A weighted scoring function combines subject overlap, availability, location proximity, and rating, then feeds a max-heap to rank tutors per student. Hash maps cache repeated lookups so the rank surfaces instantly even as the student tunes their filters live.

highlights.title: Availability & booking
highlights.lede: Where the match becomes a session.
highlights.body: Tutors set availability through a calendar surface; students see only the slots that survive their own constraints. The booking flow writes through to SQL atomically, so two students can never book the same slot — even if they click at the same time.

challenges.title: Designing a matching score that survives ties
challenges.body: Multi-criteria weighting always produces ties at the top of the heap, and ties looked random to the user. The fix was a secondary deterministic sort (rating, then alphabetical) baked into the comparator so the same student always saw the same first tutor — predictability turned out to matter more than statistical purity.

challenges.title: Separating student and tutor flows without duplicating the app
challenges.body: Two parallel UIs would have meant two parallel bugs. The build used a single set of JavaFX scenes with role-aware view models — same components, different data — so a fix to a shared card was a single edit, not two. The lesson generalized: shared primitives, divergent flows.

tech: Atomic SQL writes for double-book prevention
